Residents of these fiefs, called '''Jinpuko''' (神封戸), served the shrine by paying taxes or labor duties and taking on roles such as {{ill|Hōri|ja|祝 (神職)}}.
Generally, '''{{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|ja|神戸 (民戸)}}''' (神戸, also read ''kōbe'') or '''Kanbe''' (神部, also read ''kamube'') is considered synonymous with Jinpu. However, differences in their origins and roles during the early stages are noted, with their convergence believed to have occurred during the late {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.
Many {{ill|Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)}} trace their origins to the pre-{{ill|Taika Reform|ja|大化の改新}} period as part of the {{ill|Be-min system|ja|部民}}. On the other hand, Jinpu is thought to have been established as part of the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}’s {{ill|Shokufu system|ja|食封制度}}. Nevertheless, Jinpu is not explicitly stipulated in legal codes such as the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}. It is only mentioned in the annotations (朱説) within the {{ill|Ryō no Shūge|ja|令集解}} under the chapter on stipends for officials. Therefore, Jinpu-related rules were likely determined by {{ill|Daijōkan directives|ja|太政官符}} and {{ill|Daijōkan letters|ja|太政官牒}}.
Initially, unlike Jinko, Jinpu fief residents were obligated to contribute only corvée labor and taxes as per the provisions of the {{ill|Fuyaku Code|ja|賦役令}}, paying half of their land tax to the shrine and the rest to the state. However, in the 11th year of {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} (739 CE), all taxes from Jinpu fiefs were directed to the shrine, effectively eliminating tax differences between Jinpu and Jinko. Around this time, shrines were granted {{ill|Japanese court rank|ja|位階|Japanese court rank=Japanese court rank|lt=ranks}} and corresponding fiefs, similar to officials, as part of the {{ill|Iifū system|ja|位封制度}}. However, records such as the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} reveal instances where these fiefs were not fully distributed.
By the early Heian period, distinctions between Jinpu and Jinko had faded, leading to their treatment as one entity. In the {{ill|Shinshō Gyōchokushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄}}, a {{ill|Daijōkan letter|ja|太政官牒}} from the 1st year of {{ill|Daidō|ja|大同}} (806 CE) consolidates them, indicating that they were regarded as identical by that time.
== References == * Mizuno Ryūtarō, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Dictionary of Japanese History|ja|国史大辞典}} Vol. 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986). ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Tokinoya Shigeru, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Heian Period Historical Dictionary|ja|平安時代史事典}} (Kadokawa Shoten, 1994). ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7.

{{Referencing method|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 05:11 (UTC)}} '''Jinpu''' (神封) refers to {{ill|fiefs|ja|封戸}} granted to {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} as donations.
Residents of these fiefs, called '''Jinpuko''' (神封戸), served the shrine by paying taxes or labor duties and taking on roles such as {{ill|Hōri|ja|祝 (神職)}}.
Generally, '''{{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|ja|神戸 (民戸)}}''' (神戸, also read ''kōbe'') or '''Kanbe''' (神部, also read ''kamube'') is considered synonymous with Jinpu. However, differences in their origins and roles during the early stages are noted, with their convergence believed to have occurred during the late {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.
Many {{ill|Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)}} trace their origins to the pre-{{ill|Taika Reform|ja|大化の改新}} period as part of the {{ill|Be-min system|ja|部民}}. On the other hand, Jinpu is thought to have been established as part of the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}’s {{ill|Shokufu system|ja|食封制度}}. Nevertheless, Jinpu is not explicitly stipulated in legal codes such as the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}. It is only mentioned in the annotations (朱説) within the {{ill|Ryō no Shūge|ja|令集解}} under the chapter on stipends for officials. Therefore, Jinpu-related rules were likely determined by {{ill|Daijōkan directives|ja|太政官符}} and {{ill|Daijōkan letters|ja|太政官牒}}.
Initially, unlike Jinko, Jinpu fief residents were obligated to contribute only corvée labor and taxes as per the provisions of the {{ill|Fuyaku Code|ja|賦役令}}, paying half of their land tax to the shrine and the rest to the state. However, in the 11th year of {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} (739 CE), all taxes from Jinpu fiefs were directed to the shrine, effectively eliminating tax differences between Jinpu and Jinko. Around this time, shrines were granted {{ill|Japanese court rank|ja|位階|Japanese court rank=Japanese court rank|lt=ranks}} and corresponding fiefs, similar to officials, as part of the {{ill|Iifū system|ja|位封制度}}. However, records such as the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} reveal instances where these fiefs were not fully distributed.
By the early Heian period, distinctions between Jinpu and Jinko had faded, leading to their treatment as one entity. In the {{ill|Shinshō Gyōchokushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄}}, a {{ill|Daijōkan letter|ja|太政官牒}} from the 1st year of {{ill|Daidō|ja|大同}} (806 CE) consolidates them, indicating that they were regarded as identical by that time.

revision.text:
{{Referencing method|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 05:11 (UTC)}} '''Jinpu''' (神封) refers to {{ill|fiefs|ja|封戸}} granted to {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} as donations.
Residents of these fiefs, called '''Jinpuko''' (神封戸), served the shrine by paying taxes or labor duties and taking on roles such as {{ill|Hōri|ja|祝 (神職)}}.
Generally, '''{{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|ja|神戸 (民戸)}}''' (神戸, also read ''kōbe'') or '''Kanbe''' (神部, also read ''kamube'') is considered synonymous with Jinpu. However, differences in their origins and roles during the early stages are noted, with their convergence believed to have occurred during the late {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.
Many {{ill|Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)}} trace their origins to the pre-{{ill|Taika Reform|ja|大化の改新}} period as part of the {{ill|Be-min system|ja|部民}}. On the other hand, Jinpu is thought to have been established as part of the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}’s {{ill|Shokufu system|ja|食封制度}}. Nevertheless, Jinpu is not explicitly stipulated in legal codes such as the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}. It is only mentioned in the annotations (朱説) within the {{ill|Ryō no Shūge|ja|令集解}} under the chapter on stipends for officials. Therefore, Jinpu-related rules were likely determined by {{ill|Daijōkan directives|ja|太政官符}} and {{ill|Daijōkan letters|ja|太政官牒}}.
Initially, unlike Jinko, Jinpu fief residents were obligated to contribute only corvée labor and taxes as per the provisions of the {{ill|Fuyaku Code|ja|賦役令}}, paying half of their land tax to the shrine and the rest to the state. However, in the 11th year of {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} (739 CE), all taxes from Jinpu fiefs were directed to the shrine, effectively eliminating tax differences between Jinpu and Jinko. Around this time, shrines were granted {{ill|Japanese court rank|ja|位階|Japanese court rank=Japanese court rank|lt=ranks}} and corresponding fiefs, similar to officials, as part of the {{ill|Iifū system|ja|位封制度}}. However, records such as the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} reveal instances where these fiefs were not fully distributed.
By the early Heian period, distinctions between Jinpu and Jinko had faded, leading to their treatment as one entity. In the {{ill|Shinshō Gyōchokushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄}}, a {{ill|Daijōkan letter|ja|太政官牒}} from the 1st year of {{ill|Daidō|ja|大同}} (806 CE) consolidates them, indicating that they were regarded as identical by that time.
== References == * Mizuno Ryūtarō, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Dictionary of Japanese History|ja|国史大辞典}} Vol. 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986). ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Tokinoya Shigeru, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Heian Period Historical Dictionary|ja|平安時代史事典}} (Kadokawa Shoten, 1994). ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7.== See Also == * [[Atashigami]] (他神) ''' Amatsu Yashiro (天社) ''' Kamudokoro (神地) ''' Kamube (神部) ''' Kunitsu Yashiro (國社) * {{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|ja|神戸 (民戸)}}

Many {{ill|Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)}} trace their origins to the pre-{{ill|Taika Reform|ja|大化の改新}} period as part of the {{ill|Be-min system|ja|部民}}. On the other hand, Jinpu is thought to have been established as part of the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}’s {{ill|Shokufu system|ja|食封制度}}. revision.comment: Text replacement - "== See Also ==" to " == See Also! =="
revision.text:
{{Referencing method|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 05:11 (UTC)}} '''Jinpu''' (神封) refers to {{ill|fiefs|ja|封戸}} granted to {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} as donations.
Residents of these fiefs, called '''Jinpuko''' (神封戸), served the shrine by paying taxes or labor duties and taking on roles such as {{ill|Hōri|ja|祝 (神職)}}.
Generally, '''{{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|ja|神戸 (民戸)}}''' (神戸, also read ''kōbe'') or '''Kanbe''' (神部, also read ''kamube'') is considered synonymous with Jinpu. However, differences in their origins and roles during the early stages are noted, with their convergence believed to have occurred during the late {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.
Many {{ill|Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)}} trace their origins to the pre-{{ill|Taika Reform|ja|大化の改新}} period as part of the {{ill|Be-min system|ja|部民}}. On the other hand, Jinpu is thought to have been established as part of the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}’s {{ill|Shokufu system|ja|食封制度}}. Nevertheless, Jinpu is not explicitly stipulated in legal codes such as the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}. It is only mentioned in the annotations (朱説) within the {{ill|Ryō no Shūge|ja|令集解}} under the chapter on stipends for officials. Therefore, Jinpu-related rules were likely determined by {{ill|Daijōkan directives|ja|太政官符}} and {{ill|Daijōkan letters|ja|太政官牒}}.
Initially, unlike Jinko, Jinpu fief residents were obligated to contribute only corvée labor and taxes as per the provisions of the {{ill|Fuyaku Code|ja|賦役令}}, paying half of their land tax to the shrine and the rest to the state. However, in the 11th year of {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} (739 CE), all taxes from Jinpu fiefs were directed to the shrine, effectively eliminating tax differences between Jinpu and Jinko. Around this time, shrines were granted {{ill|Japanese court rank|ja|位階|Japanese court rank=Japanese court rank|lt=ranks}} and corresponding fiefs, similar to officials, as part of the {{ill|Iifū system|ja|位封制度}}. However, records such as the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} reveal instances where these fiefs were not fully distributed.
By the early Heian period, distinctions between Jinpu and Jinko had faded, leading to their treatment as one entity. In the {{ill|Shinshō Gyōchokushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄}}, a {{ill|Daijōkan letter|ja|太政官牒}} from the 1st year of {{ill|Daidō|ja|大同}} (806 CE) consolidates them, indicating that they were regarded as identical by that time.
== References == * Mizuno Ryūtarō, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Dictionary of Japanese History|ja|国史大辞典}} Vol. 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986). ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Tokinoya Shigeru, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Heian Period Historical Dictionary|ja|平安時代史事典}} (Kadokawa Shoten, 1994). ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. == See Also! == * [[Atashigami]] (他神) ''' Amatsu Yashiro (天社) ''' Kamudokoro (神地) ''' Kamube (神部) ''' Kunitsu Yashiro (國社) * {{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|ja|神戸 (民戸)}}
{{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Ritsuryō system in Japan]]

{{Referencing method|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 05:11 (UTC)}} '''Jinpu''' (神封) refers to {{ill|fiefs|ja|封戸}} granted to {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} as donations.
Residents of these fiefs, called '''Jinpuko''' (神封戸), served the shrine by paying taxes or labor duties and taking on roles such as {{ill|Hōri|ja|祝 (神職)}}.
Generally, '''{{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|ja|神戸 (民戸)}}''' (神戸, also read ''kōbe'') or '''Kanbe''' (神部, also read ''kamube'') is considered synonymous with Jinpu. However, differences in their origins and roles during the early stages are noted, with their convergence believed to have occurred during the late {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.
Many {{ill|Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)}} trace their origins to the pre-{{ill|Taika Reform|ja|大化の改新}} period as part of the {{ill|Be-min system|ja|部民}}. On the other hand, Jinpu is thought to have been established as part of the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}’s {{ill|Shokufu system|ja|食封制度}}. Nevertheless, Jinpu is not explicitly stipulated in legal codes such as the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}. It is only mentioned in the annotations (朱説) within the {{ill|Ryō no Shūge|ja|令集解}} under the chapter on stipends for officials. Therefore, Jinpu-related rules were likely determined by {{ill|Daijōkan directives|ja|太政官符}} and {{ill|Daijōkan letters|ja|太政官牒}}.
Initially, unlike Jinko, Jinpu fief residents were obligated to contribute only corvée labor and taxes as per the provisions of the {{ill|Fuyaku Code|ja|賦役令}}, paying half of their land tax to the shrine and the rest to the state. However, in the 11th year of {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} (739 CE), all taxes from Jinpu fiefs were directed to the shrine, effectively eliminating tax differences between Jinpu and Jinko. Around this time, shrines were granted {{ill|Japanese court rank|ja|位階|Japanese court rank=Japanese court rank|lt=ranks}} and corresponding fiefs, similar to officials, as part of the {{ill|Iifū system|ja|位封制度}}. However, records such as the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} reveal instances where these fiefs were not fully distributed.
By the early Heian period, distinctions between Jinpu and Jinko had faded, leading to their treatment as one entity. In the {{ill|Shinshō Gyōchokushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄}}, a {{ill|Daijōkan letter|ja|太政官牒}} from the 1st year of {{ill|Daidō|ja|大同}} (806 CE) consolidates them, indicating that they were regarded as identical by that time.
== References == * Mizuno Ryūtarō, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Dictionary of Japanese History|ja|国史大辞典}} Vol. 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986). ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Tokinoya Shigeru, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Heian Period Historical Dictionary|ja|平安時代史事典}} (Kadokawa Shoten, 1994). ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. == See Also == * [[Atashigami]] (他神) ''' Amatsu Yashiro (天社) ''' Kamudokoro (神地) ''' Kamube (神部) ''' Kunitsu Yashiro (國社) * {{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|ja|神戸 (民戸)}}

However, records such as the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} reveal instances where these fiefs were not fully distributed.
By the early Heian period, distinctions between Jinpu and Jinko had faded, leading to their treatment as one entity. In the {{ill|Shinshō Gyōchokushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄}}, a {{ill|Daijōkan letter|ja|太政官牒}} from the 1st year of {{ill|Daidō|ja|大同}} (806 CE) consolidates them, indicating that they were regarded as identical by that time.
== References == * Mizuno Ryūtarō, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Dictionary of Japanese History|ja|国史大辞典}} Vol. 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986). ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Tokinoya Shigeru, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Heian Period Historical Dictionary|ja|平安時代史事典}} (Kadokawa Shoten, 1994). ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. == See Also == * [[Atashigami]] (他神) ''' Amatsu Yashiro (天社) ''' Kamudokoro (神地) ''' Kamube (神部) ''' Kunitsu Yashiro (國社) * {{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|ja|神戸 (民戸)}}

{{Referencing method|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 05:11 (UTC)}} '''Jinpu''' (神封) refers to {{ill|fiefs|ja|封戸}} granted to {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} as donations.
Residents of these fiefs, called '''Jinpuko''' (神封戸), served the shrine by paying taxes or labor duties and taking on roles such as {{ill|Hōri|ja|祝 (神職)}}.
Generally, '''{{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|ja|神戸 (民戸)}}''' (神戸, also read ''kōbe'') or '''Kanbe''' (神部, also read ''kamube'') is considered synonymous with Jinpu. However, differences in their origins and roles during the early stages are noted, with their convergence believed to have occurred during the late {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.
Many {{ill|Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)}} trace their origins to the pre-{{ill|Taika Reform|ja|大化の改新}} period as part of the {{ill|Be-min system|ja|部民}}. On the other hand, Jinpu is thought to have been established as part of the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}’s {{ill|Shokufu system|ja|食封制度}}. Nevertheless, Jinpu is not explicitly stipulated in legal codes such as the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}. It is only mentioned in the annotations (朱説) within the {{ill|Ryō no Shūge|ja|令集解}} under the chapter on stipends for officials. Therefore, Jinpu-related rules were likely determined by {{ill|Daijōkan directives|ja|太政官符}} and {{ill|Daijōkan letters|ja|太政官牒}}.
Initially, unlike Jinko, Jinpu fief residents were obligated to contribute only corvée labor and taxes as per the provisions of the {{ill|Fuyaku Code|ja|賦役令}}, paying half of their land tax to the shrine and the rest to the state. However, in the 11th year of {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} (739 CE), all taxes from Jinpu fiefs were directed to the shrine, effectively eliminating tax differences between Jinpu and Jinko. Around this time, shrines were granted {{ill|Japanese court rank|ja|位階|Japanese court rank=Japanese court rank|lt=ranks}} and corresponding fiefs, similar to officials, as part of the {{ill|Iifū system|ja|位封制度}}. However, records such as the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} reveal instances where these fiefs were not fully distributed.
By the early Heian period, distinctions between Jinpu and Jinko had faded, leading to their treatment as one entity. In the {{ill|Shinshō Gyōchokushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄}}, a {{ill|Daijōkan letter|ja|太政官牒}} from the 1st year of {{ill|Daidō|ja|大同}} (806 CE) consolidates them, indicating that they were regarded as identical by that time.
== References == * Mizuno Ryūtarō, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Dictionary of Japanese History|ja|国史大辞典}} Vol. 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986). ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Tokinoya Shigeru, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Heian Period Historical Dictionary|ja|平安時代史事典}} (Kadokawa Shoten, 1994). ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. == See Also == * [[Atashigami]] (他神) ''' Amatsu Yashiro (天社) ''' Kamudokoro (神地) ''' Kamube (神部) ''' Kunitsu Yashiro (國社) * {{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|ja|神戸 (民戸)}}

revision.comment: Bot: Reformatted page - moved categories, interwikis, DEFAULTSORT, and removed duplicates.
revision.text:
{{Referencing method|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 05:11 (UTC)}} '''Jinpu''' (神封) refers to {{ill|fiefs|ja|封戸}} granted to {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} as donations.
Residents of these fiefs, called '''Jinpuko''' (神封戸), served the shrine by paying taxes or labor duties and taking on roles such as {{ill|Hōri|ja|祝 (神職)}}.
Generally, '''{{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|ja|神戸 (民戸)}}''' (神戸, also read ''kōbe'') or '''Kanbe''' (神部, also read ''kamube'') is considered synonymous with Jinpu. However, differences in their origins and roles during the early stages are noted, with their convergence believed to have occurred during the late {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} to the early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.
Many {{ill|Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)}} trace their origins to the pre-{{ill|Taika Reform|ja|大化の改新}} period as part of the {{ill|Be-min system|ja|部民}}. On the other hand, Jinpu is thought to have been established as part of the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}’s {{ill|Shokufu system|ja|食封制度}}. Nevertheless, Jinpu is not explicitly stipulated in legal codes such as the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}}. It is only mentioned in the annotations (朱説) within the {{ill|Ryō no Shūge|ja|令集解}} under the chapter on stipends for officials. Therefore, Jinpu-related rules were likely determined by {{ill|Daijōkan directives|ja|太政官符}} and {{ill|Daijōkan letters|ja|太政官牒}}.
Initially, unlike Jinko, Jinpu fief residents were obligated to contribute only corvée labor and taxes as per the provisions of the {{ill|Fuyaku Code|ja|賦役令}}, paying half of their land tax to the shrine and the rest to the state. However, in the 11th year of {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} (739 CE), all taxes from Jinpu fiefs were directed to the shrine, effectively eliminating tax differences between Jinpu and Jinko. Around this time, shrines were granted {{ill|Japanese court rank|ja|位階|Japanese court rank=Japanese court rank|lt=ranks}} and corresponding fiefs, similar to officials, as part of the {{ill|Iifū system|ja|位封制度}}. However, records such as the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} reveal instances where these fiefs were not fully distributed.
By the early Heian period, distinctions between Jinpu and Jinko had faded, leading to their treatment as one entity. In the {{ill|Shinshō Gyōchokushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄}}, a {{ill|Daijōkan letter|ja|太政官牒}} from the 1st year of {{ill|Daidō|ja|大同}} (806 CE) consolidates them, indicating that they were regarded as identical by that time.
== References == * Mizuno Ryūtarō, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Dictionary of Japanese History|ja|国史大辞典}} Vol. 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986). ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Tokinoya Shigeru, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Heian Period Historical Dictionary|ja|平安時代史事典}} (Kadokawa Shoten, 1994). ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. == See Also == * [[Atashigami]] (他神) ''' Amatsu Yashiro (天社) ''' Kamudokoro (神地) ''' Kamube (神部) ''' Kunitsu Yashiro (國社) * {{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|ja|神戸 (民戸)}}
{{shinto-stub}}

Residents of these fiefs, called '''Jinpuko''' (神封戸), served the shrine by paying taxes or labor duties and taking on roles such as {{ill|Hōri|ja|祝 (神職)|12=draft}}.
Generally, '''{{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=draft}}''' (神戸, also read ''kōbe'') or '''Kanbe''' (神部, also read ''kamube'') is considered synonymous with Jinpu. However, differences in their origins and roles during the early stages are noted, with their convergence believed to have occurred during the late {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}} to the early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}.
Many {{ill|Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=draft}} trace their origins to the pre-{{ill|Taika Reform|ja|大化の改新|12=draft}} period as part of the {{ill|Be-min system|ja|部民|12=draft}}. On the other hand, Jinpu is thought to have been established as part of the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|12=draft}}’s {{ill|Shokufu system|ja|食封制度|12=draft}}. Nevertheless, Jinpu is not explicitly stipulated in legal codes such as the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=draft}}. It is only mentioned in the annotations (朱説) within the {{ill|Ryō no Shūge|ja|令集解|12=draft}} under the chapter on stipends for officials. Therefore, Jinpu-related rules were likely determined by {{ill|Daijōkan directives|ja|太政官符|12=draft}} and {{ill|Daijōkan letters|ja|太政官牒|12=draft}}.
Initially, unlike Jinko, Jinpu fief residents were obligated to contribute only corvée labor and taxes as per the provisions of the {{ill|Fuyaku Code|ja|賦役令|12=draft}}, paying half of their land tax to the shrine and the rest to the state. However, in the 11th year of {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平|12=draft}} (739 CE), all taxes from Jinpu fiefs were directed to the shrine, effectively eliminating tax differences between Jinpu and Jinko. Around this time, shrines were granted {{ill|Japanese court rank|ja|位階|Japanese court rank=Japanese court rank|lt=ranks|12=draft}} and corresponding fiefs, similar to officials, as part of the {{ill|Iifū system|ja|位封制度|12=draft}}. However, records such as the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|12=draft}} reveal instances where these fiefs were not fully distributed.
By the early Heian period, distinctions between Jinpu and Jinko had faded, leading to their treatment as one entity. In the {{ill|Shinshō Gyōchokushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|12=draft}}, a {{ill|Daijōkan letter|ja|太政官牒|12=draft}} from the 1st year of {{ill|Daidō|ja|大同|12=draft}} (806 CE) consolidates them, indicating that they were regarded as identical by that time.
== References == * Mizuno Ryūtarō, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Dictionary of Japanese History|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft}} Vol. 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986). ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Tokinoya Shigeru, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Heian Period Historical Dictionary|ja|平安時代史事典|12=draft}} (Kadokawa Shoten, 1994). ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. == See Also == * [[Atashigami]] (他神) ''' Amatsu Yashiro (天社) ''' Kamudokoro (神地) ''' Kamube (神部) ''' Kunitsu Yashiro (國社) * {{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=draft}}

revision.text:
{{参照方法|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 05:11 (UTC)}} '''神封'''（じんぷ）とは、[[神社]]に対して[[寄進]]された[[封戸]]。
神封の住民は租税や課役を神社に納めたり、[[祝 (神職)|祝]]などの役職を務めることで神社に奉仕した。こうした住民を'''神封戸'''（じんふこ）と呼ぶ。
一般的には'''[[神戸 (民戸) |神戸]]'''（じんこ、かんべ）または'''神部'''（かんべ、かむべ）と同義とされるが、その成立時期や初期における役割には差異があったと考えられ、両者の混合が進んだのは、[[奈良時代]]後期から[[平安時代]]初期にかけてと考えられている。
神戸の多くが[[大化]]前代に遡ることが出来ると考えられる所謂[[部民]]であったと考えられるのに対して、神封は[[律令制]]による[[食封]]制度の一環として成立したと考えられている。ただし、[[養老令]]などに直接規定したものは無く、『[[令集解]]』の[[禄令]]令条外条における朱説（[[明法家]]による注釈とされる注記）に示されているに過ぎない。従って神封に関する規定は[[太政官符]]・[[太政官牒]]で定められていた。
当初は神封戸は神戸とは違い、[[賦役令]]の封戸に関する規定によって庸調及び課役のみを負担して田租の半分は神社に残りは国家に納めていたと考えられている。ところが、[[天平]]11年（[[739年]]）に封戸の租は全て封主（神封の場合は神社）に納めることとされ、神戸との税制的な違いが消滅した。また、この頃より神社に対しても[[官人]]と同様に[[位階]]を授けて[[位封]]を支給する制度が行われるようになり、官人の位階と同様の位封が授けられた（ただし、『[[続日本紀]]』などの記録によれば、全額が支給されていない例もあったことが判明する）。これも神封として扱われた。
平安時代初期になると、実質的な差異が失われた神戸と神封が混同されて同一に扱われるようになった。『[[新抄格勅符抄]]』に所収されている[[大同 (日本)|大同]]元年（[[806年]]）の太政官牒では一括して扱われ、この頃には両者が同一のものとみなされていたとみられている。
==参考文献== *水野柳太郎「神封」（『国史大辞典 7』（吉川弘文館、1986年） ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4） *時野谷滋「神封」（『平安時代史事典』（角川書店、1994年） ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7）
==関連項目== *[[他神]]（あたしがみ） **天社（あまつやしろ） **神地（かむどころ） **神部（かむべ） **國社（くにつやしろ） *[[神戸 (民戸)|神戸]]（じんこ、かんべ）
{{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しんふ}} [[Category:日本の律令制]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神封]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1689534]] Wikidata name: jinpu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencing method|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 05:11 (UTC)}} '''Jinpu''' (神封) refers to {{ill|fiefs|ja|封戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fiefs|qq=|1=fuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458391}} granted to [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] as donations.
Residents of these fiefs, called '''Jinpuko''' (神封戸), served the shrine by paying taxes or labor duties and taking on roles such as {{ill|Hōri|ja|祝 (神職)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōri|qq=}}.
Generally, '''{{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|ja|神戸 (民戸)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|qq=}}''' (神戸, also read ''kōbe'') or '''Kanbe''' (神部, also read ''kamube'') is considered synonymous with Jinpu. However, differences in their origins and roles during the early stages are noted, with their convergence believed to have occurred during the late [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] to the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
Many {{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|lt=Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}} trace their origins to the pre-[[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]] period as part of the {{ill|Be-min system|ja|部民|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Be-min system|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 部民制|1=bemin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2892644}}. On the other hand, Jinpu is thought to have been established as part of the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]]’s {{ill|Shokufu system|ja|食封制度|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokufu system|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Nevertheless, Jinpu is not explicitly stipulated in legal codes such as the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}. It is only mentioned in the annotations (朱説) within the {{ill|Ryō no Shūge|ja|令集解|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryō no Shūge|qq=|1=Ryōshūge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378321}} under the chapter on stipends for officials. Therefore, Jinpu-related rules were likely determined by {{ill|Daijōkan directives|ja|太政官符|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan directives|qq=|1=Daijō-kan-pu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444013}} and {{ill|Daijōkan letters|ja|太政官牒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan letters|qq=|1=Daijō-kan chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444011}}.
Initially, unlike Jinko, Jinpu fief residents were obligated to contribute only corvée labor and taxes as per the provisions of the {{ill|Fuyaku Code|ja|賦役令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuyaku Code|qq=|1=Fuyaku Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283213}}, paying half of their land tax to the shrine and the rest to the state. However, in the 11th year of [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]] (739 CE), all taxes from Jinpu fiefs were directed to the shrine, effectively eliminating tax differences between Jinpu and Jinko. Around this time, shrines were granted {{ill|Japanese court Rank|ja|位階|Japanese court Rank=Japanese court Rank|lt=Ranks|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese court Rank|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=court rank in Japan|WD=Q99196082}} and corresponding fiefs, similar to officials, as part of the {{ill|Iifū system|ja|位封制度|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iifū system|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, records such as the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] reveal instances where these fiefs were not fully distributed.
By the early Heian period, distinctions between Jinpu and Jinko had faded, leading to their treatment as one entity. In the {{ill|Shinshō Gyōchokushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshō Gyōchokushō|qq=|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11502312}}, a {{ill|Daijōkan letter|ja|太政官牒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan letter|qq=|1=Daijō-kan chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444011}} from the 1st year of [[:en:Datong (disambiguation)|Daidō]] (806 CE) consolidates them, indicating that they were regarded as identical by that time.
== References == * Mizuno Ryūtarō, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Dictionary of Japanese History|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dictionary of Japanese History|qq=|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}} Vol. 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986). ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Tokinoya Shigeru, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Heian Period Historical Dictionary|ja|平安時代史事典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Period Historical Dictionary|qq=|1=Heian Jidaishi Jiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482399}} (Kadokawa Shoten, 1994). ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. == See Also == * [[Atashigami]] (他神) ''' Amatsu Yashiro (天社) ''' Kamudokoro (神地) ''' Kamube (神部) ''' Kunitsu Yashiro (國社) * {{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|ja|神戸 (民戸)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|qq=}}

<!--Shinpo--><!--Shinpo--><!--Shinpo--><!--Shinpo--><!--Shinpo--><!--Shinpo--><!--Shinpo--><!--Shinpo--><!--Shinpo--><!--Shinpo--> [[Category:Ritsuryō System of Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q1689534}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神封]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1689534]] Wikidata name: jinpu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencing method|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 05:11 (UTC)}} '''Jinpu''' (神封) refers to {{ill|fiefs|ja|封戸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fiefs|qq=|1=fuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458391}} granted to [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] as donations.
Residents of these fiefs, called '''Jinpuko''' (神封戸), served the shrine by paying taxes or labor duties and taking on roles such as {{ill|Hōri|ja|祝 (神職)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōri|qq=}}.
Generally, '''{{ill|Kanbe (social class)|ja|神戸 (民戸)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanbe (social class)|qq=}}''' (神戸, also read ''kōbe'') or '''Kanbe''' (神部, also read ''kamube'') is considered synonymous with Jinpu. However, differences in their origins and roles during the early stages are noted, with their convergence believed to have occurred during the late [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] to the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
Many {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|lt=Kanbe (social class)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}} trace their origins to the pre-[[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]] period as part of the {{ill|Be-min system|ja|部民|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Be-min system|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 部民制|1=bemin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2892644}}. On the other hand, Jinpu is thought to have been established as part of the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]]’s {{ill|Shokufu system|ja|食封制度|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokufu system|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Nevertheless, Jinpu is not explicitly stipulated in legal codes such as the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}. It is only mentioned in the annotations (朱説) within the {{ill|Ryō no Shūge|ja|令集解|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryō no Shūge|qq=|1=Ryōshūge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378321}} under the chapter on stipends for officials. Therefore, Jinpu-related rules were likely determined by {{ill|Daijōkan directives|ja|太政官符|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan directives|qq=|1=Daijō-kan-pu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444013}} and {{ill|Daijōkan letters|ja|太政官牒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan letters|qq=|1=Daijō-kan chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444011}}.
Initially, unlike Jinko, Jinpu fief residents were obligated to contribute only corvée labor and taxes as per the provisions of the {{ill|Fuyaku Code|ja|賦役令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuyaku Code|qq=|1=Fuyaku Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283213}}, paying half of their land tax to the shrine and the rest to the state. However, in the 11th year of [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]] (739 CE), all taxes from Jinpu fiefs were directed to the shrine, effectively eliminating tax differences between Jinpu and Jinko. Around this time, shrines were granted {{ill|Japanese court Rank|ja|位階|Japanese court Rank=Japanese court Rank|lt=Ranks|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese court Rank|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=court rank in Japan|WD=Q99196082}} and corresponding fiefs, similar to officials, as part of the {{ill|Iifū system|ja|位封制度|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iifū system|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, records such as the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] reveal instances where these fiefs were not fully distributed.
By the early Heian period, distinctions between Jinpu and Jinko had faded, leading to their treatment as one entity. In the {{ill|Shinshō Gyōchokushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshō Gyōchokushō|qq=|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11502312}}, a {{ill|Daijōkan letter|ja|太政官牒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan letter|qq=|1=Daijō-kan chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444011}} from the 1st year of [[:en:Datong (disambiguation)|Daidō]] (806 CE) consolidates them, indicating that they were regarded as identical by that time.
== References == * Mizuno Ryūtarō, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Dictionary of Japanese History|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dictionary of Japanese History|qq=|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}} Vol. 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986). ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Tokinoya Shigeru, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Heian Period Historical Dictionary|ja|平安時代史事典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Period Historical Dictionary|qq=|1=Heian Jidaishi Jiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482399}} (Kadokawa Shoten, 1994). ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. == See Also == * [[Atashigami]] (他神) ''' Amatsu Yashiro (天社) ''' Kamudokoro (神地) ''' Kamube (神部) ''' Kunitsu Yashiro (國社) * {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|ja|神戸 (民戸)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanbe (social class)|qq=}}

ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Tokinoya Shigeru, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Heian Period Historical Dictionary|ja|平安時代史事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Period Historical Dictionary|1=Heian Jidaishi Jiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482399}} (Kadokawa Shoten, 1994). ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. == See Also == * [[Atashigami]] (他神) ''' Amatsu Yashiro (天社) ''' Kamudokoro (神地) ''' Kamube (神部) ''' Kunitsu Yashiro (國社) * {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanbe (social class)|qq=}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神封]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1689534]] Wikidata name: jinpu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencing method|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 05:11 (UTC)}} '''Jinpu''' (神封) refers to {{ill|fiefs|ja|封戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fiefs|1=fuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458391}} granted to [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] as donations.
Residents of these fiefs, called '''Jinpuko''' (神封戸), served the shrine by paying taxes or labor duties and taking on roles such as {{ill|Hōri|ja|祝 (神職)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōri|qq=}}.
Generally, '''{{ill|Kanbe (social class)|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanbe (social class)|qq=}}''' (神戸, also read ''kōbe'') or '''Kanbe''' (神部, also read ''kamube'') is considered synonymous with Jinpu. However, differences in their origins and roles during the early stages are noted, with their convergence believed to have occurred during the late [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] to the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
Many {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|lt=Kanbe (social class)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}} trace their origins to the pre-[[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]] period as part of the {{ill|Be-min system|ja|部民|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Be-min system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 部民制|1=bemin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2892644}}. On the other hand, Jinpu is thought to have been established as part of the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]]’s {{ill|Shokufu system|ja|食封制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokufu system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Nevertheless, Jinpu is not explicitly stipulated in legal codes such as the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}. It is only mentioned in the annotations (朱説) within the {{ill|Ryō no Shūge|ja|令集解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryō no Shūge|1=Ryōshūge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378321}} under the chapter on stipends for officials. Therefore, Jinpu-related rules were likely determined by {{ill|Daijōkan directives|ja|太政官符|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan directives|1=Daijō-kan-pu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444013}} and {{ill|Daijōkan letters|ja|太政官牒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan letters|1=Daijō-kan chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444011}}.
Initially, unlike Jinko, Jinpu fief residents were obligated to contribute only corvée labor and taxes as per the provisions of the {{ill|Fuyaku Code|ja|賦役令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuyaku Code|1=Fuyaku Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283213}}, paying half of their land tax to the shrine and the rest to the state. However, in the 11th year of [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]] (739 CE), all taxes from Jinpu fiefs were directed to the shrine, effectively eliminating tax differences between Jinpu and Jinko. Around this time, shrines were granted {{ill|Japanese court Rank|ja|位階|Japanese court Rank=Japanese court Rank|lt=Ranks|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese court Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=court rank in Japan|WD=Q99196082}} and corresponding fiefs, similar to officials, as part of the {{ill|Iifū system|ja|位封制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iifū system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, records such as the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] reveal instances where these fiefs were not fully distributed.
By the early Heian period, distinctions between Jinpu and Jinko had faded, leading to their treatment as one entity. In the {{ill|Shinshō Gyōchokushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshō Gyōchokushō|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11502312}}, a {{ill|Daijōkan letter|ja|太政官牒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan letter|1=Daijō-kan chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444011}} from the 1st year of [[:en:Datong (disambiguation)|Daidō]] (806 CE) consolidates them, indicating that they were regarded as identical by that time.
== References == * Mizuno Ryūtarō, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Dictionary of Japanese History|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dictionary of Japanese History|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}} Vol. 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986). ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Tokinoya Shigeru, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Heian Period Historical Dictionary|ja|平安時代史事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Period Historical Dictionary|1=Heian Jidaishi Jiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482399}} (Kadokawa Shoten, 1994). ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. == See Also == * [[Atashigami]] (他神) ''' Amatsu Yashiro (天社) ''' Kamudokoro (神地) ''' Kamube (神部) ''' Kunitsu Yashiro (國社) * {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanbe (social class)|qq=}}
{{shinto-stub}}

In the {{ill|Shinshō Gyōchokushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshō Gyōchokushō|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11502312}}, a {{ill|Daijōkan letter|ja|太政官牒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan letter|1=Daijō-kan chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444011}} from the 1st year of [[:en:Datong (disambiguation)|Daidō]] (806 CE) consolidates them, indicating that they were regarded as identical by that time.
== References == * Mizuno Ryūtarō, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Dictionary of Japanese History|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dictionary of Japanese History|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}} Vol. 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986). ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Tokinoya Shigeru, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Heian Period Historical Dictionary|ja|平安時代史事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Period Historical Dictionary|1=Heian Jidaishi Jiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482399}} (Kadokawa Shoten, 1994). ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. == See Also == * [[Atashigami]] (他神) ''' Amatsu Yashiro (天社) ''' Kamudokoro (神地) ''' Kamube (神部) ''' Kunitsu Yashiro (國社) * {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanbe (social class)|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神封]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1689534]] Wikidata name: jinpu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencing method|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 05:11 (UTC)}} '''Jinpu''' (神封) refers to {{ill|fiefs|ja|封戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fiefs|1=fuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458391}} granted to [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] as donations.
Residents of these fiefs, called '''Jinpuko''' (神封戸), served the shrine by paying taxes or labor duties and taking on roles such as {{ill|Hōri|ja|祝 (神職)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōri|qq=}}.
Generally, '''{{ill|Kanbe (social class)|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanbe (social class)|qq=}}''' (神戸, also read ''kōbe'') or '''Kanbe''' (神部, also read ''kamube'') is considered synonymous with Jinpu. However, differences in their origins and roles during the early stages are noted, with their convergence believed to have occurred during the late [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] to the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
Many {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|lt=Kanbe (social class)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}} trace their origins to the pre-[[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]] period as part of the {{ill|Be-min system|ja|部民|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Be-min system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 部民制|1=bemin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2892644}}. On the other hand, Jinpu is thought to have been established as part of the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]]’s {{ill|Shokufu system|ja|食封制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokufu system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Nevertheless, Jinpu is not explicitly stipulated in legal codes such as the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}. It is only mentioned in the annotations (朱説) within the {{ill|Ryō no Shūge|ja|令集解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryō no Shūge|1=Ryōshūge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378321}} under the chapter on stipends for officials. Therefore, Jinpu-related rules were likely determined by {{ill|Daijōkan directives|ja|太政官符|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan directives|1=Daijō-kan-pu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444013}} and {{ill|Daijōkan letters|ja|太政官牒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan letters|1=Daijō-kan chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444011}}.
Initially, unlike Jinko, Jinpu fief residents were obligated to contribute only corvée labor and taxes as per the provisions of the {{ill|Fuyaku Code|ja|賦役令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuyaku Code|1=Fuyaku Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283213}}, paying half of their land tax to the shrine and the rest to the state. However, in the 11th year of [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]] (739 CE), all taxes from Jinpu fiefs were directed to the shrine, effectively eliminating tax differences between Jinpu and Jinko. Around this time, shrines were granted {{ill|Japanese court Rank|ja|位階|Japanese court Rank=Japanese court Rank|lt=Ranks|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese court Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=court rank in Japan|WD=Q99196082}} and corresponding fiefs, similar to officials, as part of the {{ill|Iifū system|ja|位封制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iifū system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, records such as the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] reveal instances where these fiefs were not fully distributed.
By the early Heian period, distinctions between Jinpu and Jinko had faded, leading to their treatment as one entity. In the {{ill|Shinshō Gyōchokushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshō Gyōchokushō|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11502312}}, a {{ill|Daijōkan letter|ja|太政官牒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan letter|1=Daijō-kan chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444011}} from the 1st year of [[:en:Datong (disambiguation)|Daidō]] (806 CE) consolidates them, indicating that they were regarded as identical by that time.
== References == * Mizuno Ryūtarō, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Dictionary of Japanese History|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dictionary of Japanese History|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}} Vol. 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986). ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Tokinoya Shigeru, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Heian Period Historical Dictionary|ja|平安時代史事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Period Historical Dictionary|1=Heian Jidaishi Jiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482399}} (Kadokawa Shoten, 1994). Initially, unlike Jinko, Jinpu fief residents were obligated to contribute only corvée labor and taxes as per the provisions of the {{ill|Fuyaku Code|ja|賦役令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuyaku Code|1=Fuyaku Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283213}}, paying half of their land tax to the shrine and the rest to the state. However, in the 11th year of [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]] (739 CE), all taxes from Jinpu fiefs were directed to the shrine, effectively eliminating tax differences between Jinpu and Jinko. Around this time, shrines were granted {{ill|Japanese court Rank|ja|位階|Japanese court Rank=Japanese court Rank|lt=Ranks|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese court Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=court rank in Japan|WD=Q99196082}} and corresponding fiefs, similar to officials, as part of the {{ill|Iifū system|ja|位封制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iifū system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, records such as the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] reveal instances where these fiefs were not fully distributed.
By the early Heian period, distinctions between Jinpu and Jinko had faded, leading to their treatment as one entity. In the {{ill|Shinshō Gyōchokushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshō Gyōchokushō|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11502312}}, a {{ill|Daijōkan letter|ja|太政官牒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan letter|1=Daijō-kan chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444011}} from the 1st year of [[:en:Datong (disambiguation)|Daidō]] (806 CE) consolidates them, indicating that they were regarded as identical by that time.
== References == * Mizuno Ryūtarō, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Dictionary of Japanese History|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dictionary of Japanese History|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}} Vol. 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986). ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Tokinoya Shigeru, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Heian Period Historical Dictionary|ja|平安時代史事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Period Historical Dictionary|1=Heian Jidaishi Jiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482399}} (Kadokawa Shoten, 1994). ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. == See Also == * [[Atashigami]] (他神) ''' Amatsu Yashiro (天社) ''' Kamudokoro (神地) ''' Kamube (神部) ''' Kunitsu Yashiro (國社) * {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanbe (social class)|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神封]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1689534]] Wikidata name: jinpu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencing method|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 05:11 (UTC)}} '''Jinpu''' (神封) refers to {{ill|fiefs|ja|封戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fiefs|1=fuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458391}} granted to [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] as donations.
Residents of these fiefs, called '''Jinpuko''' (神封戸), served the shrine by paying taxes or labor duties and taking on roles such as {{ill|Hōri|ja|祝 (神職)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōri|qq=}}.
Generally, '''{{ill|Kanbe (social class)|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanbe (social class)|qq=}}''' (神戸, also read ''kōbe'') or '''Kanbe''' (神部, also read ''kamube'') is considered synonymous with Jinpu. However, differences in their origins and roles during the early stages are noted, with their convergence believed to have occurred during the late [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] to the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
Many {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|lt=Kanbe (social class)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}} trace their origins to the pre-[[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]] period as part of the {{ill|Be-min system|ja|部民|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Be-min system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 部民制|1=bemin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2892644}}. On the other hand, Jinpu is thought to have been established as part of the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]]’s {{ill|Shokufu system|ja|食封制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokufu system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Nevertheless, Jinpu is not explicitly stipulated in legal codes such as the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}. It is only mentioned in the annotations (朱説) within the {{ill|Ryō no Shūge|ja|令集解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryō no Shūge|1=Ryōshūge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378321}} under the chapter on stipends for officials. Therefore, Jinpu-related rules were likely determined by {{ill|Daijōkan directives|ja|太政官符|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan directives|1=Daijō-kan-pu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444013}} and {{ill|Daijōkan letters|ja|太政官牒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan letters|1=Daijō-kan chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444011}}.
Initially, unlike Jinko, Jinpu fief residents were obligated to contribute only corvée labor and taxes as per the provisions of the {{ill|Fuyaku Code|ja|賦役令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuyaku Code|1=Fuyaku Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283213}}, paying half of their land tax to the shrine and the rest to the state. However, in the 11th year of [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]] (739 CE), all taxes from Jinpu fiefs were directed to the shrine, effectively eliminating tax differences between Jinpu and Jinko. Around this time, shrines were granted {{ill|Japanese court Rank|ja|位階|Japanese court Rank=Japanese court Rank|lt=Ranks|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese court Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=court rank in Japan|WD=Q99196082}} and corresponding fiefs, similar to officials, as part of the {{ill|Iifū system|ja|位封制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iifū system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, records such as the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] reveal instances where these fiefs were not fully distributed.
By the early Heian period, distinctions between Jinpu and Jinko had faded, leading to their treatment as one entity. In the {{ill|Shinshō Gyōchokushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshō Gyōchokushō|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11502312}}, a {{ill|Daijōkan letter|ja|太政官牒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan letter|1=Daijō-kan chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444011}} from the 1st year of [[:en:Datong (disambiguation)|Daidō]] (806 CE) consolidates them, indicating that they were regarded as identical by that time.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神封]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1689534]] Wikidata name: jinpu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencing method|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 05:11 (UTC)}} '''Jinpu''' (神封) refers to {{ill|fiefs|ja|封戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fiefs|1=fuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458391}} granted to [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] as donations.
Residents of these fiefs, called '''Jinpuko''' (神封戸), served the shrine by paying taxes or labor duties and taking on roles such as {{ill|Hōri|ja|祝 (神職)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōri|qq=}}.
Generally, '''{{ill|Kanbe (social class)|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanbe (social class)|qq=}}''' (神戸, also read ''kōbe'') or '''Kanbe''' (神部, also read ''kamube'') is considered synonymous with Jinpu. However, differences in their origins and roles during the early stages are noted, with their convergence believed to have occurred during the late [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] to the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
Many {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|lt=Kanbe (social class)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}} trace their origins to the pre-[[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]] period as part of the {{ill|Be-min system|ja|部民|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Be-min system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 部民制|1=bemin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2892644}}. On the other hand, Jinpu is thought to have been established as part of the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]]’s {{ill|Shokufu system|ja|食封制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokufu system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Nevertheless, Jinpu is not explicitly stipulated in legal codes such as the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}. It is only mentioned in the annotations (朱説) within the {{ill|Ryō no Shūge|ja|令集解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryō no Shūge|1=Ryōshūge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378321}} under the chapter on stipends for officials. Therefore, Jinpu-related rules were likely determined by {{ill|Daijōkan directives|ja|太政官符|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan directives|1=Daijō-kan-pu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444013}} and {{ill|Daijōkan letters|ja|太政官牒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan letters|1=Daijō-kan chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444011}}.
Initially, unlike Jinko, Jinpu fief residents were obligated to contribute only corvée labor and taxes as per the provisions of the {{ill|Fuyaku Code|ja|賦役令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuyaku Code|1=Fuyaku Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283213}}, paying half of their land tax to the shrine and the rest to the state. However, in the 11th year of [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]] (739 CE), all taxes from Jinpu fiefs were directed to the shrine, effectively eliminating tax differences between Jinpu and Jinko. Around this time, shrines were granted {{ill|Japanese court Rank|ja|位階|Japanese court Rank=Japanese court Rank|lt=Ranks|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese court Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=court rank in Japan|WD=Q99196082}} and corresponding fiefs, similar to officials, as part of the {{ill|Iifū system|ja|位封制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iifū system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, records such as the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] reveal instances where these fiefs were not fully distributed.
By the early Heian period, distinctions between Jinpu and Jinko had faded, leading to their treatment as one entity. In the {{ill|Shinshō Gyōchokushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshō Gyōchokushō|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11502312}}, a {{ill|Daijōkan letter|ja|太政官牒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan letter|1=Daijō-kan chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444011}} from the 1st year of [[:en:Datong (disambiguation)|Daidō]] (806 CE) consolidates them, indicating that they were regarded as identical by that time.
== References == * Mizuno Ryūtarō, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Dictionary of Japanese History|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dictionary of Japanese History|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}} Vol. 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986). ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Tokinoya Shigeru, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Heian Period Historical Dictionary|ja|平安時代史事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Period Historical Dictionary|1=Heian Jidaishi Jiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482399}} (Kadokawa Shoten, 1994). ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. == See Also == * [[Atashigami]] (他神) ''' Amatsu Yashiro (天社) ''' Kamudokoro (神地) ''' Kamube (神部) ''' Kunitsu Yashiro (國社) * {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanbe (social class)|qq=}}
{{shinto-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|神封|version=65283959|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Completion tier 1]] }}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神封]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神封]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神封]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1689534]] Wikidata name: jinpu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencing method|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 05:11 (UTC)}} '''Jinpu''' (神封) refers to {{ill|fiefs|ja|封戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fiefs|1=fuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458391}} granted to [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] as donations.
Residents of these fiefs, called '''Jinpuko''' (神封戸), served the shrine by paying taxes or labor duties and taking on roles such as {{ill|Hōri|ja|祝 (神職)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōri|qq=}}.
Generally, '''{{ill|Kanbe (social class)|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanbe (social class)|qq=}}''' (神戸, also read ''kōbe'') or '''Kanbe''' (神部, also read ''kamube'') is considered synonymous with Jinpu. However, differences in their origins and roles during the early stages are noted, with their convergence believed to have occurred during the late [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] to the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
Many {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|lt=Kanbe (social class)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}} trace their origins to the pre-[[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]] period as part of the {{ill|Be-min system|ja|部民|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Be-min system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 部民制|1=bemin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2892644}}. On the other hand, Jinpu is thought to have been established as part of the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]]’s {{ill|Shokufu system|ja|食封制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokufu system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Nevertheless, Jinpu is not explicitly stipulated in legal codes such as the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}. It is only mentioned in the annotations (朱説) within the {{ill|Ryō no Shūge|ja|令集解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryō no Shūge|1=Ryōshūge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378321}} under the chapter on stipends for officials. Therefore, Jinpu-related rules were likely determined by {{ill|Daijōkan directives|ja|太政官符|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan directives|1=Daijō-kan-pu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444013}} and {{ill|Daijōkan letters|ja|太政官牒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan letters|1=Daijō-kan chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444011}}.
Initially, unlike Jinko, Jinpu fief residents were obligated to contribute only corvée labor and taxes as per the provisions of the {{ill|Fuyaku Code|ja|賦役令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuyaku Code|1=Fuyaku Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283213}}, paying half of their land tax to the shrine and the rest to the state. However, in the 11th year of [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]] (739 CE), all taxes from Jinpu fiefs were directed to the shrine, effectively eliminating tax differences between Jinpu and Jinko. Around this time, shrines were granted {{ill|Japanese court Rank|ja|位階|Japanese court Rank=Japanese court Rank|lt=Ranks|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese court Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=court rank in Japan|WD=Q99196082}} and corresponding fiefs, similar to officials, as part of the {{ill|Iifū system|ja|位封制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iifū system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, records such as the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] reveal instances where these fiefs were not fully distributed.
By the early Heian period, distinctions between Jinpu and Jinko had faded, leading to their treatment as one entity. In the {{ill|Shinshō Gyōchokushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshō Gyōchokushō|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11502312}}, a {{ill|Daijōkan letter|ja|太政官牒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan letter|1=Daijō-kan chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444011}} from the 1st year of [[:en:Datong (disambiguation)|Daidō]] (806 CE) consolidates them, indicating that they were regarded as identical by that time.
== References == * Mizuno Ryūtarō, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Dictionary of Japanese History|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dictionary of Japanese History|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}} Vol. 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986). ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Tokinoya Shigeru, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Heian Period Historical Dictionary|ja|平安時代史事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Period Historical Dictionary|1=Heian Jidaishi Jiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482399}} (Kadokawa Shoten, 1994). ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. == See Also == * [[Atashigami]] (他神) ''' Amatsu Yashiro (天社) ''' Kamudokoro (神地) ''' Kamube (神部) ''' Kunitsu Yashiro (國社) * {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanbe (social class)|qq=}}
{{shinto-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|神封|version=65283959|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories|
}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神封]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神封]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神封]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1689534]] Wikidata name: jinpu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencing method|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 05:11 (UTC)}} '''Jinpu''' (神封) refers to {{ill|fiefs|ja|封戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fiefs|1=fuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458391}} granted to [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] as donations.
Residents of these fiefs, called '''Jinpuko''' (神封戸), served the shrine by paying taxes or labor duties and taking on roles such as {{ill|Hōri|ja|祝 (神職)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōri|qq=}}.
Generally, '''{{ill|Kanbe (social class)|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanbe (social class)|qq=}}''' (神戸, also read ''kōbe'') or '''Kanbe''' (神部, also read ''kamube'') is considered synonymous with Jinpu. However, differences in their origins and roles during the early stages are noted, with their convergence believed to have occurred during the late [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] to the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
Many {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|lt=Kanbe (social class)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}} trace their origins to the pre-[[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]] period as part of the {{ill|Be-min system|ja|部民|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Be-min system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 部民制|1=bemin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2892644}}. On the other hand, Jinpu is thought to have been established as part of the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]]’s {{ill|Shokufu system|ja|食封制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokufu system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Nevertheless, Jinpu is not explicitly stipulated in legal codes such as the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}. It is only mentioned in the annotations (朱説) within the {{ill|Ryō no Shūge|ja|令集解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryō no Shūge|1=Ryōshūge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378321}} under the chapter on stipends for officials. Therefore, Jinpu-related rules were likely determined by {{ill|Daijōkan directives|ja|太政官符|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan directives|1=Daijō-kan-pu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444013}} and {{ill|Daijōkan letters|ja|太政官牒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan letters|1=Daijō-kan chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444011}}.
Initially, unlike Jinko, Jinpu fief residents were obligated to contribute only corvée labor and taxes as per the provisions of the {{ill|Fuyaku Code|ja|賦役令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuyaku Code|1=Fuyaku Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283213}}, paying half of their land tax to the shrine and the rest to the state. However, in the 11th year of [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō]] (739 CE), all taxes from Jinpu fiefs were directed to the shrine, effectively eliminating tax differences between Jinpu and Jinko. Around this time, shrines were granted {{ill|Japanese court Rank|ja|位階|Japanese court Rank=Japanese court Rank|lt=Ranks|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese court Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=court rank in Japan|WD=Q99196082}} and corresponding fiefs, similar to officials, as part of the {{ill|Iifū system|ja|位封制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iifū system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, records such as the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] reveal instances where these fiefs were not fully distributed.
By the early Heian period, distinctions between Jinpu and Jinko had faded, leading to their treatment as one entity. In the {{ill|Shinshō Gyōchokushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshō Gyōchokushō|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11502312}}, a {{ill|Daijōkan letter|ja|太政官牒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan letter|1=Daijō-kan chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444011}} from the 1st year of [[:en:Datong (disambiguation)|Daidō]] (806 CE) consolidates them, indicating that they were regarded as identical by that time.
== References == * Mizuno Ryūtarō, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Dictionary of Japanese History|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dictionary of Japanese History|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}} Vol. 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986). ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Tokinoya Shigeru, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Heian Period Historical Dictionary|ja|平安時代史事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Period Historical Dictionary|1=Heian Jidaishi Jiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482399}} (Kadokawa Shoten, 1994). ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. == See Also == * [[Atashigami]] (他神) ''' Amatsu Yashiro (天社) ''' Kamudokoro (神地) ''' Kamube (神部) ''' Kunitsu Yashiro (國社) * {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanbe (social class)|qq=}}
{{shinto-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|神封|version=65283959|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:神封]]

In the {{ill|Shinshō Gyōchokushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshō Gyōchokushō|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11502312}}, a {{ill|Daijōkan letter|ja|太政官牒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan letter|1=Daijō-kan chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444011}} from the 1st year of {{ill|Datong (disambiguation)|en|Datong (disambiguation)|WD=Q295082|lt=Daidō}} (806 CE) consolidates them, indicating that they were regarded as identical by that time.
== References == * Mizuno Ryūtarō, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Dictionary of Japanese History|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dictionary of Japanese History|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}} Vol. 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986). ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Tokinoya Shigeru, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Heian Period Historical Dictionary|ja|平安時代史事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Period Historical Dictionary|1=Heian Jidaishi Jiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482399}} (Kadokawa Shoten, 1994). ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. == See Also == * [[Atashigami]] (他神) ''' Amatsu Yashiro (天社) ''' Kamudokoro (神地) ''' Kamube (神部) ''' Kunitsu Yashiro (國社) * {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanbe (social class)|qq=}}
{{shinto-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神封]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神封]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神封]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1689534]] Wikidata name: jinpu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencing method|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 05:11 (UTC)}} '''Jinpu''' (神封) refers to {{ill|fiefs|ja|封戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fiefs|1=fuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458391}} granted to {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines}} as donations.
Residents of these fiefs, called '''Jinpuko''' (神封戸), served the shrine by paying taxes or labor duties and taking on roles such as {{ill|Hōri|ja|祝 (神職)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōri|qq=}}.
Generally, '''{{ill|Kanbe (social class)|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanbe (social class)|qq=}}''' (神戸, also read ''kōbe'') or '''Kanbe''' (神部, also read ''kamube'') is considered synonymous with Jinpu. However, differences in their origins and roles during the early stages are noted, with their convergence believed to have occurred during the late {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} to the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.
Many {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|lt=Kanbe (social class)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}} trace their origins to the pre-{{ill|Taika Reform|en|Taika Reform|WD=Q570140|lt=Taika Reform}} period as part of the {{ill|Be-min system|ja|部民|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Be-min system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 部民制|1=bemin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2892644}}. On the other hand, Jinpu is thought to have been established as part of the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system}}’s {{ill|Shokufu system|ja|食封制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokufu system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Nevertheless, Jinpu is not explicitly stipulated in legal codes such as the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}. It is only mentioned in the annotations (朱説) within the {{ill|Ryō no Shūge|ja|令集解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryō no Shūge|1=Ryōshūge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378321}} under the chapter on stipends for officials. Therefore, Jinpu-related rules were likely determined by {{ill|Daijōkan directives|ja|太政官符|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan directives|1=Daijō-kan-pu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444013}} and {{ill|Daijōkan letters|ja|太政官牒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan letters|1=Daijō-kan chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444011}}.
Initially, unlike Jinko, Jinpu fief residents were obligated to contribute only corvée labor and taxes as per the provisions of the {{ill|Fuyaku Code|ja|賦役令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuyaku Code|1=Fuyaku Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283213}}, paying half of their land tax to the shrine and the rest to the state. However, in the 11th year of {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|WD=Q1199879|lt=Tenpyō}} (739 CE), all taxes from Jinpu fiefs were directed to the shrine, effectively eliminating tax differences between Jinpu and Jinko. Around this time, shrines were granted {{ill|Japanese court Rank|ja|位階|Japanese court Rank=Japanese court Rank|lt=Ranks|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese court Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=court rank in Japan|WD=Q99196082}} and corresponding fiefs, similar to officials, as part of the {{ill|Iifū system|ja|位封制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iifū system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, records such as the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}} reveal instances where these fiefs were not fully distributed.
By the early Heian period, distinctions between Jinpu and Jinko had faded, leading to their treatment as one entity. In the {{ill|Shinshō Gyōchokushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshō Gyōchokushō|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11502312}}, a {{ill|Daijōkan letter|ja|太政官牒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan letter|1=Daijō-kan chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444011}} from the 1st year of {{ill|Datong (disambiguation)|en|Datong (disambiguation)|WD=Q295082|lt=Daidō}} (806 CE) consolidates them, indicating that they were regarded as identical by that time.
== References == * Mizuno Ryūtarō, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Dictionary of Japanese History|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dictionary of Japanese History|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}} Vol. 7 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986). ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Tokinoya Shigeru, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Heian Period Historical Dictionary|ja|平安時代史事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Period Historical Dictionary|1=Heian Jidaishi Jiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482399}} (Kadokawa Shoten, 1994). ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. == See Also == * [[Atashigami]] (他神) ''' Amatsu Yashiro (天社) ''' Kamudokoro (神地) ''' Kamube (神部) ''' Kunitsu Yashiro (國社) * {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanbe (social class)|qq=}}

Therefore, Jinpu-related rules were likely determined by {{ill|Daijōkan directives|ja|太政官符|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan directives|1=Daijō-kan-pu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444013}} and {{ill|Daijōkan letters|ja|太政官牒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan letters|1=Daijō-kan chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444011}}.
Initially, unlike Jinko, Jinpu fief residents were obligated to contribute only corvée labor and taxes as per the provisions of the {{ill|Fuyaku Code|ja|賦役令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuyaku Code|1=Q65283213|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283213}}, paying half of their land tax to the shrine and the rest to the state. However, in the 11th year of {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|WD=Q1199879|lt=Tenpyō|1=Tenpyō}} (739 CE), all taxes from Jinpu fiefs were directed to the shrine, effectively eliminating tax differences between Jinpu and Jinko. Around this time, shrines were granted {{ill|Japanese court Rank|ja|位階|Japanese court Rank=Japanese court Rank|lt=Ranks|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese court Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Court Ranks in Japan|WD=Q99196082}} and corresponding fiefs, similar to officials, as part of the {{ill|Iifū system|ja|位封制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iifū system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, records such as the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi|1=Shoku Nihongi}} reveal instances where these fiefs were not fully distributed.
By the early Heian period, distinctions between Jinpu and Jinko had faded, leading to their treatment as one entity. In the {{ill|Shinshō Gyōchokushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshō Gyōchokushō|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11502312}}, a {{ill|Daijōkan letter|ja|太政官牒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan letter|1=Daijō-kan chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444011}} from the 1st year of {{ill|Datong (disambiguation)|en|Datong (disambiguation)|WD=Q295082|lt=Daidō|1=Datong (disambiguation)}} (806 CE) consolidates them, indicating that they were regarded as identical by that time.

ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Tokinoya Shigeru, "Jinpu," in {{ill|Heian Period Historical Dictionary|ja|平安時代史事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Period Historical Dictionary|1=Heian Jidaishi Jiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482399}} (Kadokawa Shoten, 1994). ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. == See Also == * [[Atashigami]] (他神) ''' Amatsu Yashiro (天社) ''' Kamudokoro (神地) ''' Kamube (神部) ''' Kunitsu Yashiro (國社) * {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanbe (social class)|qq=}}
