{{Referencing method|date=August 28, 2017 (Mon) 05:11 (UTC)}} '''Jinpu''' (神封) refers to {{ill|fiefs|ja|封戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fiefs|1=Fūko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458391}} granted to {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines|1=Shinto shrine}} as donations.
Residents of these fiefs, called '''Jinpuko''' (神封戸), served the shrine by paying taxes or labor duties and taking on roles such as {{ill|Hōri|ja|祝 (神職)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōri|qq=}}.
Generally, '''{{ill|Kanbe (social class)|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanbe (social class)|qq=}}''' (神戸, also read ''kōbe'') or '''Kanbe''' (神部, also read ''kamube'') is considered synonymous with Jinpu. However, differences in their origins and roles during the early stages are noted, with their convergence believed to have occurred during the late {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} to the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.
Many {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|lt=Kanbe (social class)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}} trace their origins to the pre-{{ill|Taika Reform|en|Taika Reform|WD=Q570140|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika Reform}} period as part of the {{ill|Be-min system|ja|部民|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Be-min system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 部民制|1=Bemin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2892644}}. On the other hand, Jinpu is thought to have been established as part of the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō}}’s {{ill|Shokufu system|ja|食封制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokufu system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Nevertheless, Jinpu is not explicitly stipulated in legal codes such as the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}}. It is only mentioned in the annotations (朱説) within the {{ill|Ryō no Shūge|ja|令集解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryō no Shūge|1=Ryōshūge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378321}} under the chapter on stipends for officials. Therefore, Jinpu-related rules were likely determined by {{ill|Daijōkan directives|ja|太政官符|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan directives|1=Daijō-kan-pu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444013}} and {{ill|Daijōkan letters|ja|太政官牒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan letters|1=Daijō-kan chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444011}}.
Initially, unlike Jinko, Jinpu fief residents were obligated to contribute only corvée labor and taxes as per the provisions of the {{ill|Fuyaku Code|ja|賦役令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuyaku Code|1=Q65283213|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65283213}}, paying half of their land tax to the shrine and the rest to the state. However, in the 11th year of {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|WD=Q1199879|lt=Tenpyō|1=Tenpyō}} (739 CE), all taxes from Jinpu fiefs were directed to the shrine, effectively eliminating tax differences between Jinpu and Jinko. Around this time, shrines were granted {{ill|Japanese court Rank|ja|位階|Japanese court Rank=Japanese court Rank|lt=Ranks|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese court Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Court Ranks in Japan|WD=Q99196082}} and corresponding fiefs, similar to officials, as part of the {{ill|Iifū system|ja|位封制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iifū system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, records such as the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi|1=Shoku Nihongi}} reveal instances where these fiefs were not fully distributed.
By the early Heian period, distinctions between Jinpu and Jinko had faded, leading to their treatment as one entity. In the {{ill|Shinshō Gyōchokushō|ja|新抄格勅符抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinshō Gyōchokushō|1=Shinshō Kyakuchokufushō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11502312}}, a {{ill|Daijōkan letter|ja|太政官牒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōkan letter|1=Daijō-kan chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444011}} from the 1st year of {{ill|Datong (disambiguation)|en|Datong (disambiguation)|WD=Q295082|lt=Daidō|1=Datong (disambiguation)}} (806 CE) consolidates them, indicating that they were regarded as identical by that time.
